text: I notice when someone's handshake lingers a beat too long or ends a beat too soon.
I can sense when a group chat message was drafted and redrafted before being sent.
I pick up on the way someone subtly angles their body toward the person they most want approval from.
I detect when someone uses a nickname versus a full name to signal the current state of a relationship.
I notice when the rhythm of someone's texting changes, even slightly, and read meaning into it.
I can tell when someone is including unnecessary details in a story to justify themselves.
I sense when a person is choosing to sit in a specific seat because of who is already sitting where.
I notice when someone matches the volume of the loudest person in the room to assert themselves.
I pick up on the difference between someone who is quiet by nature and someone who has been silenced by the group.
I detect when someone's laughter is slightly delayed, as if they are calculating whether to join in.
I notice when a person crosses their arms specifically in response to what was just said, not because they are cold.
I can tell when two people have had a private conversation before a group meeting based on how they interact.
I sense when someone brings up a third party's name to indirectly communicate something to the person they are speaking with.
I pick up on the way someone's voice gets slightly higher when they are not fully confident in what they are saying.
I notice when a person offers to help not out of generosity but to position themselves as indispensable.
I detect when someone deliberately mispronounces a word or name to signal familiarity or distance.
I notice when someone repeats a phrase another person used earlier to build rapport or to mock them.
I can tell when a meeting is happening for show rather than for any real decision-making.
I sense when someone introduces a topic casually but actually considers it extremely important.
I pick up on when a group collectively decides to pretend something did not happen.
I notice when someone slightly raises their eyebrows in a micro-reaction before composing their face.
I detect the difference between someone sharing personal information to build trust and someone doing it to create a sense of obligation.
I notice when a person touches their face or neck during a conversation as a self-soothing gesture.
I can tell when someone is feigning ignorance to get someone else to reveal information.
I sense when the energy of a room drops the moment a particular person starts speaking.
I pick up on when someone is strategically agreeing with everyone to avoid being pinned to a position.
I notice when a person checks their phone not because they have a notification but because they want to disengage.
I detect when someone answers a different question than the one that was asked to steer the conversation.
I notice the way certain people in a group look to one specific person before reacting.
I can sense when someone is holding a grudge even though they are acting normally.
I pick up on when a person's enthusiasm for a plan is masking their resignation about it.
I notice when someone speeds up their speech to get through a point before they can be interrupted.
I detect when a gift or favor comes with unspoken strings attached.
I notice when a person brings up old information at a strategic moment to shift the dynamics of a conversation.
I sense when someone is listening with the intent to find a weakness in what is being said.
I pick up on the way people's footsteps change when they are approaching someone they are nervous around.
I notice when someone's eye contact becomes slightly more intense because they want to be believed.
I can tell when a couple is mid-argument even though they are being perfectly civil in public.
I sense when a team member is withdrawing their investment in a project through subtle disengagement.
I notice when someone uses the phrase 'to be honest' as a signal that previous statements may not have been.
I detect when someone is watching a conversation from the periphery with more interest than they are showing.
I pick up on when a person's compliment is actually a way of drawing attention to something they disapprove of.
I notice the way someone's posture changes depending on whether they are speaking to a peer or someone they perceive as above or below them.
I sense when a person tells a self-deprecating joke to preempt a criticism they expect.
I can tell when someone is pretending not to have heard something so they do not have to respond to it.
I notice when a person swallows or clears their throat before saying something that is difficult for them.
I detect when someone's goodbye carries finality versus when it is a casual see-you-later.
I pick up on the specific way that silence falls after someone says something that crossed a line.
I notice when a person's tone becomes overly measured, as if they are carefully controlling their emotions.
I sense when two people are communicating through a third person who does not realize they are being used as a channel.
I can tell when someone volunteers information to create an impression of transparency while still hiding the important parts.
I notice when a group's laughter is directed at someone rather than shared with them.
I detect when someone is offering praise specifically because they want something in return.
I pick up on when a person references a shared memory to remind the other person of their bond.
I notice when someone adopts a casual posture to deliberately lower the perceived stakes of a conversation.
I sense when a question is being asked to confirm something the asker already knows, as a test.
I detect when a friend group has a designated peacekeeper who steps in at predictable moments.
I notice the tiny pause before someone says a name, as if deciding whether to include or exclude that person from the narrative.
I can tell when someone is narrating their own actions aloud to shape how others perceive what they are doing.
I pick up on the difference between a sigh of relief and a sigh of frustration even when they sound almost identical.
I notice when someone glances at the clock or door during a conversation, signaling they have mentally left.
I sense when a group is tacitly agreeing to lower the emotional register of a conversation to keep things safe.
I detect when someone emphasizes a particular word to send a message that only certain listeners will catch.
I notice when a person's typing speed in a live chat changes in response to the emotional content of the discussion.
I pick up on when someone deliberately mentions a deadline or constraint to create urgency and shift the power balance.
I can tell when someone is smiling with their mouth but not with the muscles around their eyes.
I notice when a person starts a sentence, stops, and then restarts with different words because they caught a social risk.
I sense the precise moment when a debate stops being about the topic and becomes about winning.
I detect when someone uses vague language like 'some people think' to express their own opinion without owning it.
I notice when the pitch of a room changes because one dominant personality entered or left.
I pick up on when a person lingers after a group disperses because they want a private word with someone specific.
I sense when someone gives a longer answer than the question warranted because they are nervous.
I notice when a person arranges their belongings on a table to claim space or establish boundaries.
I detect when someone uses humor to say something true that they could not say seriously.
I can tell when a conversation is being held in a specific location because that location sends a message.
I notice when a person's warmth toward someone else cools by a barely perceptible degree.
I pick up on when someone is nodding along without actually agreeing.
I sense when an invitation was extended to one person but worded to include the group so it would not seem exclusive.
I detect when someone brings up a tangential topic to distract from something that was about to be discussed.
I notice the micro-adjustments people make to their clothing or hair when someone they want to impress is nearby.
I can tell from the way two people greet each other how long it has been since they last spoke and how that gap felt.
I sense when someone is using a story about another person to indirectly talk about themselves.
I notice when a person offers unsolicited advice to subtly establish their expertise.
I pick up on the difference between someone who pauses to think and someone who pauses for dramatic effect.
I detect when a group member's contribution is being quietly dismissed by the way others respond to it.
I notice when someone shifts from using 'I' to using 'we' to distribute responsibility for what they are about to say.
I sense when a conversation partner is mirroring my energy not out of genuine rapport but as a social technique.
I can tell when someone has been wanting to bring up a topic for a while and is finally finding their opening.
I notice when a person's vocal cadence shifts to match the person they are trying to connect with.
I pick up on when a compliment about one person is actually a veiled criticism of someone else present.
I detect when someone positions themselves physically between two people to mediate or interrupt a connection.
I notice when someone's reply to a group message is really only intended for one person in the thread.
I sense when a person mentions their credentials or background at a socially calculated moment.
I can tell when someone is keeping a conversation going longer than natural because they are avoiding being alone.
I notice when a person tilts their head while listening because they are genuinely trying to understand versus when they tilt it to appear attentive.
I pick up on the way someone chooses to introduce two people and what that framing reveals about their view of each.
I detect when a person's written punctuation changes to signal irritation, such as periods becoming curt.
I notice when someone deliberately echoes the vocabulary of the most powerful person in the room.
I sense when a shared meal or coffee is actually a pretext for a conversation someone needs to have.
I can feel when a social gathering has an invisible guest list that determines who was and was not expected.
I notice when someone's response time to messages changes as a reflection of their shifting feelings.
I pick up on when a person frames a demand as a collaborative suggestion.
I detect when someone remembers a detail about you not out of genuine care but to demonstrate attentiveness.
I notice when a speaker's gaze lingers on someone who challenged them earlier, even during an unrelated point.
I sense when someone is being conspicuously silent to make their absence from the conversation felt.
I can tell when a person adjusts their level of formality mid-conversation in response to a social cue they received.
I notice when the physical distance between two people in a conversation is slightly greater than normal and read the reason for it.
I detect when someone is curating which emotions they display and which they suppress in real time.
I pick up on when a person quotes someone else to lend authority to an opinion they are not confident enough to own.
I notice when two people have developed private shorthand or inside references that exclude others in the group.
I sense when a farewell is warm on the surface but carries relief underneath.
I can tell when someone asks how you are doing because they genuinely want to know versus when it is a formality.
I notice when a person places themselves in someone's line of sight to ensure they are seen.
I detect subtle territorial behaviors like someone adjusting objects on a shared table.
I pick up on when a person's energy dims slightly after receiving a response that was not what they hoped for.
I notice when someone includes a disclaimer before a statement because they are anticipating pushback from a specific person.
I sense the unspoken ranking of whose time is considered most valuable in a professional setting.
I can tell when someone is keeping score in a friendship based on how they reference past favors.
I notice when a group conversation has an unacknowledged moderator who is guiding the flow without being explicit about it.
I detect when a person's friendliness toward me is calibrated to what they think I can offer them.
I pick up on when someone is listening to a story not for the story itself but for what it reveals about the storyteller.
I notice when a person clenches or unclenches their hands in rhythm with the emotional intensity of a conversation.
I sense when two people are avoiding eye contact with each other even in a small group.
I can tell when a host is performing hospitality versus genuinely enjoying having guests.
I notice when someone reframes another person's idea in their own words to take partial ownership of it.
I detect when a person chooses to stand rather than sit as a way to signal they do not intend to stay long.
I pick up on when a silence after a question is the asker giving space versus the asker applying pressure.
I notice when someone's entire demeanor shifts depending on whether their supervisor is present.
I sense when a person withholds a compliment that would be natural because of a competitive dynamic.
I can tell when a conversation's real audience is not the person being spoken to but someone who is overhearing.
I notice the way people edit their stories in real time based on the reactions they are getting.
I detect when a person uses physical touch, like a pat on the back, to establish a hierarchy rather than express warmth.
I pick up on when someone exhales audibly as a way of communicating displeasure without words.
I notice when a group instinctively leaves a chair empty next to someone they find socially difficult.
I sense when a person is testing the loyalty of others by sharing a piece of gossip and watching where it goes.
I can tell when someone responds to a text with a shorter message than usual and what that brevity means.
I notice when a person's speaking pace slows because they are choosing their words around a sensitive listener.
I detect when the way someone closes a door, gently or firmly, carries emotional information.
I pick up on when a person positions a joke as if it just occurred to them even though it was planned.
I notice when a group's unspoken topic of avoidance is the most important thing no one is saying.
I sense when someone uses the word 'interesting' as a placeholder for a reaction they do not want to reveal.
I can tell when two people in a room are aware of each other even when they are not interacting.
I notice when someone volunteers to leave a room to give others privacy they did not explicitly request.
I detect when a person's social warmth has a ceiling that they never allow anyone past.
I pick up on when the timing of a message is itself the message.
I notice when someone addresses one person by name more frequently than others to draw them in or single them out.
I sense the difference between a group that is comfortable with each other and a group that is performing comfort.
I detect when a person uses the phrase 'no offense' as a signal that offense is exactly what they intend.
I notice when a conversation partner's pupils dilate or contract in response to what is being discussed.
I can tell when someone changes the subject not to avoid discomfort but to protect someone else from it.
I pick up on when a person who normally speaks freely becomes uncharacteristically cautious.
I notice when someone offers a carefully worded non-answer that sounds like an answer.
I generally do not analyze why someone chose a particular word when a simpler one would do.
I tend to assume people are in a neutral mood unless they tell me otherwise.
I do not usually notice when someone is nervous during a conversation unless they say so.
I find that I am more interested in the outcome of a discussion than in how people felt during it.
I rarely wonder what someone really meant by a particular comment.
I tend to accept invitations at face value without wondering if there is a subtext.
I do not naturally notice when someone changes their tone mid-sentence.
I find that I often have to ask people directly how they are feeling because I cannot tell.
I tend to miss the social significance of who arrives together or leaves together at an event.
I do not pick up on when someone is putting extra effort into their appearance for a specific social situation.
I rarely notice when a person's posture closes off in response to something I said.
I find that I do not register the difference between a quick hello and a deliberate greeting.
I tend not to think about why someone chose to sit where they sat.
I do not usually notice when people exchange a knowing glance.
I focus on whether a statement is true rather than on why someone chose to say it now.
I rarely consider whether the timing of a message carries meaning beyond its content.
I tend to take compliments at face value without analyzing what might be behind them.
I do not usually register shifts in formality during a conversation.
I find that I often miss when a conversation has an unspoken purpose beyond its surface topic.
I tend not to notice when someone is deliberately making themselves small in a social setting.
I rarely detect when a person's generosity has a strategic element to it.
I do not naturally sense when a friend group has an inner circle and an outer circle.
I tend to miss when someone includes me in a plan out of obligation rather than desire.
I find that I do not track who interrupted whom during a meeting.
I rarely notice when someone is holding back tears during a conversation.
I do not usually detect when a question is rhetorical versus when someone actually wants an answer.
I tend not to register when someone's reply carries a hint of sarcasm.
I find it hard to tell when someone is impressed versus when they are just being encouraging.
I do not naturally pick up on when someone changes the way they address me.
I rarely notice the social dynamics of who pays for what in a group.
I tend to focus on the information in an email rather than the tone.
I do not usually sense when someone is seeking reassurance rather than advice.
I find that I miss the unspoken rules about when it is appropriate to speak and when to stay quiet.
I tend not to notice when a person distances themselves from a previous statement in subtle ways.
I rarely detect when someone is performing agreement to keep the peace.
I do not pick up on when someone is testing my reaction before committing to a stance.
I tend to be unaware of when someone is jealous but hiding it.
I find that I do not instinctively sense when I have overstayed my welcome.
I rarely notice when someone is speaking louder than necessary to assert dominance.
I do not naturally register the social weight of who introduces whom in a new setting.
I tend to miss when a conversational pause is an invitation for me to share something personal.
I find it difficult to tell when a person's cheerfulness is forced.
I do not usually notice when someone edits their story in real time based on audience reactions.
I tend not to read meaning into the way someone signs off an email.
I rarely detect when a friendly rivalry has an edge of real competition underneath.
I do not naturally sense when someone wants to be contradicted rather than agreed with.
I tend to miss the social implications of who gets credit for an idea in a group.
I find that I do not notice when someone is performing a version of themselves for a new audience.
I rarely pick up on when a host is ready for their guests to leave.
I do not usually register when someone deliberately avoids a topic in my presence.
I tend not to notice the difference between laughter that invites me in and laughter that keeps me out.
I find that I often need to be told directly when I have accidentally offended someone.
I do not naturally sense when two people are having a power struggle disguised as polite conversation.
I tend to miss when someone frames bad news inside good news to soften the blow.
I rarely notice when someone's contribution to a conversation is being systematically overlooked.
I do not pick up on when someone places themselves physically in a position of authority.
I tend to be unaware of the subtle ways people express loyalty to each other in group settings.
I find that I do not read the social meaning into someone's choice of clothing for a particular occasion.
I rarely notice when a person uses qualifiers like 'maybe' or 'sort of' to soften an opinion they feel strongly about.
I do not usually detect when someone is using a personal anecdote as an indirect warning.
I tend not to register when a group's dynamic shifts because of a new piece of information.
I find that I generally do not notice when someone looks to another person for permission before speaking.
I rarely pick up on the social choreography that determines who walks next to whom.
I do not naturally sense when a person is gauging whether I am trustworthy before opening up.
I tend to miss the subtle ways that people signal they are on the same side.
I find it hard to detect when someone is being supportive on the surface but competitive underneath.
I do not usually notice when a text message carries emotional subtext that goes beyond the words.
I tend to be the last person to realize when a social event is winding down.
I rarely notice when someone is performing busyness to avoid a conversation.
I do not naturally track whose opinion shifted during a discussion and why.
I tend not to register when someone is slowly distancing themselves from a relationship.
I find that I miss the social meaning behind the order in which people are greeted or acknowledged.
I rarely sense when a group has an unspoken agreement about who the designated leader is.
I do not usually notice when someone answers a question for another person to control the narrative.
I tend to miss it when someone laughs at the wrong moment because they are uncomfortable.
I find that I do not register the emotional tenor of a handshake or hug.
I rarely detect when a person's interest in a conversation suddenly spikes because of something specific that was said.
I do not naturally notice when someone is being deliberately vague to maintain deniability.
I tend to focus on what was decided in a meeting rather than on the interpersonal dynamics that influenced the decision.
I find that I generally miss when someone uses my name mid-conversation to increase intimacy or pressure.
I rarely pick up on when a person's compliment is backhanded.
I do not usually sense when the mood of a text conversation shifts because of an emoji choice or its absence.
I tend not to notice when people form temporary alliances within a conversation.
I find it difficult to tell whether a group's silence indicates contentment or suppressed disagreement.
I do not naturally register when someone mirrors my body language to build rapport.
I tend to miss when a person's enthusiasm about a topic is meant to signal something about their values.
I rarely notice when someone slows down a conversation to give a quieter person space to contribute.
I find that I do not instinctively sense when someone feels overlooked in a group.
I do not pick up on when a social invitation is phrased to make declining difficult.
I tend to be unaware of the unspoken rules about what topics are acceptable at a particular gathering.
I rarely detect when someone's reaction to news is performed for the benefit of the person sharing it.
I do not naturally notice the way people position themselves during a photo to signal closeness or distance.
I tend not to register when a person volunteers less information than usual because of who is present.
I find that I am often unaware of the social cost of something I said until someone tells me later.
I rarely sense when a person is being patient with me rather than genuinely engaged.
I do not usually pick up on the difference between a question that invites discussion and one that shuts it down.
I tend to miss the social significance of who reaches out to whom first after a conflict.
I find that I am not naturally attuned to the way gossip functions as social bonding.
I rarely notice when the formality of an interaction is mismatched with the actual closeness of the relationship.
I do not naturally detect when someone's storytelling style changes depending on the status of their audience.
I tend to focus on the clarity of a message rather than on what the sender might be feeling.
I find that I miss when a person's body language tells a completely different story than their words.
I rarely sense when a social environment has an implicit dress code or behavioral code that I am violating.
I do not usually notice when someone is carefully not mentioning a person or topic that is obviously relevant.
I tend not to register the social dynamics of who gets the last word in a conversation.
I find that I generally do not notice when someone is managing my impression of a third party.
I rarely pick up on when a person is signaling discomfort through fidgeting or restlessness.
I do not naturally sense when a social ritual, like a toast or a round of introductions, carries deeper meaning than its surface function.
I tend to miss the way people calibrate how much personal information to share based on their read of the room.
I find that I do not track the unspoken turn-taking order in group conversations.
I rarely detect when someone adjusts their opinion after sensing the majority view.
I do not usually notice when a person glances at their companion for confirmation before committing to a plan.
I tend to treat silence in a conversation as simply a pause rather than as communication.
I find it hard to tell when someone is genuinely asking for my opinion versus looking for validation.
I rarely register the social significance of response time in digital conversations.
I do not naturally notice when someone is subtly coaching another person through a social situation.
I tend not to read meaning into someone choosing to communicate by text versus calling versus meeting in person.
I find that I am usually unaware of who feels most and least comfortable in a group until it becomes obvious.
I rarely notice the implicit choreography of how a group arranges itself when it walks together.
I do not pick up on when a person gives a long explanation because they sense skepticism from the listener.
I tend to focus on whether an idea is good rather than on how it was received by the group.
I find that I do not notice when someone deliberately withholds a reaction to maintain power in a conversation.
I rarely sense when a person is anchoring a negotiation through seemingly casual conversation.
I do not naturally detect when someone uses a group's shared values or language to gain credibility.
I tend to miss the social signal when someone says 'we should do this sometime' without ever intending to follow through.
I find that I am generally unaware of when people around me are managing each other's emotions.
I rarely notice when someone structures a question to guide me toward a particular answer.
I do not usually detect when someone drops a hint about what they want for a gift or occasion.
I tend not to notice when a person's energy changes after reading a message on their phone.
I find that I am usually the last to sense when a social norm has been broken.
I do not naturally pick up on the difference between someone sharing a vulnerability and someone testing how I will respond to it.
I rarely register when someone raises their eyebrows briefly to acknowledge a shared understanding.
I tend to miss when a person mentions something they are proud of in a way designed not to sound like bragging.
I find it hard to tell when someone is deferring to me out of genuine respect versus obligation.
I do not naturally notice when a conversation between two people nearby is meant for me to overhear.
I tend to be unaware of when someone is matching my energy level to put me at ease.
I rarely sense when a person's choice of words reveals their stance on an issue they have not explicitly addressed.
I do not usually register when a person responds with slightly more distance after I said something they disagreed with.
I tend not to notice when someone deliberately includes me in a conversation by directing a question my way.
I find that I miss when a person's farewell is warmer or cooler than their greeting and what that shift means.
I rarely pick up on the way someone positions their belongings as a social boundary.
I do not naturally sense when a group has collectively moved on from a topic that one person still wants to discuss.
I tend to miss it when a person's agreement comes with a subtle qualifier that changes its meaning entirely.
I find that I do not automatically sense when someone is emotionally exhausted even if they are carrying on normally.
I rarely notice when a person carefully positions a negative opinion as a question to soften the delivery.
I do not usually detect when a social environment has an unspoken expectation about reciprocity.
I tend not to notice when someone shifts their gaze away to signal they are done with a topic.
I find that I miss when someone's phrasing of a request reveals how much power they think they have in the relationship.
